# Pr 297345 Weight Prepack Int8 Smoothquant Allocator

Source type: github
Document ID: dsid_c84c8581594e47d49e82d024a6da915d
Release: v1.0.0
Benchmark: EnterpriseRAG-Bench synthetic upstream data
prepack-int8-weights-and-smoothquant-fuse-with-kernel-lane-allocator

Motivation: INT8 inference for large decoder models showed suboptimal memory layout overhead and extra dequant steps when combined with smoothquant-style per-channel scaling. This PR introduces a weight prepack path and a small kernel-lane allocator that lets the runtime fuse smoothquant dequantization into the kernel dispatch without materializing intermediate dequant tensors. Goals: 1) lower wall-clock latency for first-token and steady-state by removing memory-bound dequant copies, 2) reduce peak GPU memory for multi-tenant workloads by prepacking int8 weights in a compact layout and sharing lane metadata across batches, 3) keep compatibility with existing KV cache and continuous-batching pipelines. What changed (high-level): - New weight prepack module (src/runtime/kernel/int8_weight_prepack.cc/h) that produces a packed INT8 layout with per-channel scale/zero-point metadata optimized for our vectorized GEMM kernels. - Kernel adapter hooks (src/runtime/kernel/kernel_adapter.cc) to accept packed weights and apply smoothquant per-channel scaling on-the-fly inside the GEMM launcher when the model has smoothquant metadata. - Kernel lane allocator (src/runtime/scheduler/lane_allocator.cc/h) that assigns model weight lanes to worker threads/GPU streams so packed weights can be reused across microbatches without copying. - KV cache coordinator updates (src/runtime/scheduler/kv_cache_coordinator.cc) to pin KV cache blocks to the same lane affinity when the request uses pinned weights, avoiding cross-lane synchronization. - A shortfall fallback: when model uses dynamic shape patterns incompatible with lane reuse, runtime falls back to previous dequant-copy path automatically. Performance: microbenchmarks on a 32B decoder model (batch sizes 1..8, seq len 1..1024) show median token latency improvement of 9-16% at steady state and 18-28% for first-token latency on Ampere A100, with peak memory reduction of ~12% at high concurrency. Compatibility/risks: The change touches low-level kernel dispatch. We limited the surface by gating packed-weight paths behind a model feature flag and added runtime checks for per-channel metadata version. Rolling plan: staged canary rollout on Dedicated pools with a 2-hour canary window, monitor latency, token error-rate regressions, and memory counters. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for weight prepack and kernel adapter - [x] Integration test exercising smoothquant models through hosted API - [x] Performance bench scripts added - [x] Migration doc and feature flag gating - [x] Observability: added counters for lane cache hits/misses, packed-weight size. Related: ENG-3421 (quant kernel latency), ENG-3498 (kv cache affinity).
Marcus Li: Overall this looks solid; can you add a short docstring describing the layout produced by the prepack method? I had to trace through the code to confirm alignment guarantees.
Anika Rao: Added header docstrings and a diagram in prepack_smoothquant_notes.md per Marcus' request.
Diego Torres: The lane allocator is clever. Concern: potential starvation if many small requests contend for lanes. Suggest adding a fairness counter or aging policy.
Anika Rao: Added simple LRU aging policy and a unit test to simulate heavy small-request contention; PTAL.
Priya Nair (SRE): We need an observability signal for fallback-to-copy rate; ops will want to alert if it spikes after rollout.
Anika Rao: Added metrics runtime.quant.packed_weight_fallback_count and added to Console dashboards as part of the rollout task.
Marcus Li: CI failure on clang-format fixed in latest commit; approving pending perf numbers in canary.
Diego Torres: Verified no regression on tokenization edge-case tests; merging after canary.
